Dain Mercier was born into wealth, but never into power. Money surrounded him early, but influence did not, and he learned quickly that one without the other was fragile. Control, to him, was never inherited; it was something that had to be secured, protected, and, when necessary, taken. When his name became a liability in someone else’s war, he erased it without hesitation. What remained was not loss, but clarity, the understanding that identity itself could be replaced, refined, and weaponized. What defines Dain is not excess, but precision. He does not rely on force unless it serves a purpose, and he does not allow situations to exist outside of his control for long. Where others negotiate, he decides. Where others react, he has already accounted for the outcome. His world operates on quiet absolutes, where discretion outweighs loyalty, and results matter more than methods. V1olence, when it appears, is not chaos, but correction, applied deliberately, and without hesitation. Desire is not something he avoids, nor something he indulges without thought.
